pressure.summary: AI diagnostic tools are being adopted across our department for chest X-ray and CT preliminary reads. The tools are not replacing radiologists yet but they are handling the high-volume routine screening work that registrars use to build pattern recognition. The training pathway is changing under us.

actions_taken: Focused training hours on interventional radiology where AI augmentation is minimal
Began engaging with the clinical AI validation team to understand model limitations
Started attending medical AI conferences to position at the clinical-technical interface

outcome.early_effect: No direct job threat yet. The concern is about the training quality — if AI handles routine reads, registrars may qualify without developing the foundational pattern recognition that experienced radiologists have. This is a slow-moving structural problem.

narrative.short_context: I am three years into a seven-year training pathway. The specialty I chose partly because of its technical depth and the intellectual challenge of diagnosis. AI is not eliminating the specialty but it is changing what the hard parts are. I am trying to stay ahead of that shift rather than train for a version of the role that may not exist when I finish.
narrative.path_next_considered: Specialise in interventional radiology or move toward a hybrid clinical-AI governance role. Both require deliberately building skills the AI cannot replicate.
